aliases: Claypot Braised Catfish With Rice ( Cơm Cá Hú Kho)

english: Claypot Braised Catfish With Rice ( Cơm Cá Hú Kho)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese claypot braised catfish with rice. FOODON_03315091 (braised fish dish) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for this dish does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for braised catfish component; rice component maps to A00LC (Rice, cooked). Composite dish mapped to fish-cooked anchor as the protein-defining component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No single FoodEx2 code covers this composite Vietnamese dish. A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is used as the primary anchor. The rice component is a secondary staple. A composite FoodEx2 facet descriptor would be required for exact representation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese claypot braised catfish with rice as of 2024. SNOMED CT does not index this dish at the required specificity.

serving.description: 1 plate (1 serving of braised catfish with 1 portion of steamed white rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of approximately 350g, comprising a portion of claypot-braised catfish (approximately 120–150g) served alongside one cup of steamed white rice (approximately 180–200g).

nutrient_source.title: Claypot Braised Catfish With Rice (Cơm Cá Hú Kho) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Claypot Braised Catfish With Rice (Cơm Cá Hú Kho), cr_id G00010557, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central catfish and rice composite data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — White rice, steamed (analogous estimate for rice-dominant composite dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Claypot Braised Catfish with Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (jasmine-type), which has a published GI of approximately 72–80 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The catfish and braising liquid contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the glycemic profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (44.3g for 350g serve × 25.19g/100g = ~88.2g carbohydrate × 0.72 / 100 ≈ 32.0g GL). Fibre at 3.7g/100g provides partial modulation but does not shift the GI category.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231mg/100g is in the borderline range (200–400mg/100g); however, potassium at 327mg/100g and dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g provide meaningful offset.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce in the braising liquid to lower sodium content, and may consume this dish in moderate portions given the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio and fibre content that partially offset the sodium burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by the white rice component contributing 25.2g carbohydrate per 100g; caramel sauce (nước màu) adds additional free sugars to the braising liquid.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should significantly reduce the rice portion size, substitute brown or parboiled rice where possible, request reduced caramel sauce in the braising liquid, and increase the relative proportion of catfish to lower the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Catfish provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) which are cardioprotective; saturated fat is low (~1.2g/100g); however, sodium at 231mg/100g and the refined carbohydrate load from white rice are relevant cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the omega-3 content of catfish and the low saturated fat profile, but should request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium intake and consider reducing the rice portion to limit refined carbohydrate contribution to cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Catfish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; claypot braising avoids deep-frying and AGE formation; ginger and garlic in the braising liquid have anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of catfish and the anti-inflammatory aromatics, but should monitor tolerance to chili and other nightshade ingredients which may aggravate RA flares in sensitive individuals, and request reduced fish sauce to lower the sodium burden on inflamed joints.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Claypot Braised Catfish with Rice (Cơm Cá Hú Kho) is a traditional Vietnamese set meal consisting of a portion of steamed white jasmine rice served alongside catfish (cá hú) that has been slow-braised in a clay pot with fish sauce, caramel sauce (nước màu), garlic, ginger, shallots, chili, and black pepper. The braising process produces a deeply savoury, slightly sweet, and mildly spicy sauce that coats the fish, which becomes tender and flavourful through the low-and-slow claypot cooking method. The dish is a staple of Vietnamese home cooking and is commonly found in Vietnamese rice-set meal restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100g, with 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate, reflecting the rice-dominant composition. Dietary fibre is notable at 3.7g/100g, and potassium at 327mg/100g exceeds sodium at 231mg/100g, providing a favourable mineral ratio. The catfish component contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), which are anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective. The dish has a high glycemic index (~72) due to the white rice component, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. The claypot braising method avoids deep-frying, keeping saturated fat low at approximately 1.2g/100g. The dish is dairy-free and halal-suitable, though fish sauce may contain wheat-derived ingredients in some preparations.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010557) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central catfish and rice composite data as HPB does not separately report this field; this is documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked catfish and steamed white rice in proportions consistent with this dish type, as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This is an approximation and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese claypot braised catfish with rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database G00010557). This is noted as potentially anomalous for a fish-containing dish; catfish typically contains 50–70mg cholesterol per 100g of fish flesh. The HPB value of 0.0 may reflect the composite dish dilution by the rice component or a data entry limitation. This field should be flagged for review. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (jasmine-type) as the dominant carbohydrate source, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 5. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 (braised fish dish) is used as the closest approximate parent class. FoodEx2 A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is used as the primary anchor for the protein component. No exact composite dish code exists in either system for this Vietnamese preparation. 6. This dish is served in Singapore Vietnamese restaurants and hawker stalls; region_tag SG is preserved from the input record.
